title: Spinning a DK yarn on the spinning wheel
subtitle: Achieving consistent double-knitting weight using wraps-per-inch as a target
excerpt: DK weight yarn runs at approximately 11-14 wraps per inch. This tutorial explains how to set wheel tension and treadling speed to maintain consistent thickness throughout a bobbin, and how to use a wraps-per-inch gauge to check your progress as you spin.
sourceNotes: Yarn weight standards including wraps-per-inch conventions are documented in handspinning and knitting references. The technical method is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measure of yarn thickness: how many times a yarn wraps around a ruler or gauge in one inch. DK weight runs at 11-14 WPI; aran at 8-10 WPI; lace at 30+ WPI.

The rate at which the spinning wheel treadles are depressed. Faster treadling inserts more twist per length of yarn; slower treadling inserts less. Treadling speed and drafting speed must stay proportional.
A single-drive wheel system where a brake band on the bobbin controls uptake. Loosening the brake band reduces the pull-in and allows more control over yarn thickness.
The accumulation of spun yarn on a bobbin, spindle, or quill. Building the cop evenly prevents the bobbin from developing uneven weight distribution that can affect spinning consistency.

content.text: as a singles, approximately 16-18 WPI before plying. This tutorial assumes you are spinning a two-ply DK: each singles is spun to roughly 16-18 WPI, then two singles are plied to produce a finished 11-14 WPI yarn.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep hair and clothing clear of the drive band and flyer. Switch the wheel off before threading or adjusting tension.

content.text: (or double-drive tension if your wheel uses it): set it at the lightest tension that still draws yarn in smoothly. A heavy draw-in will pull yarn thinner than you intend. Set your
to medium: consistent speed is more important than fast spinning for consistent results.
Step 1: Spin a test length and measure

Spin 1-2 m of singles using the short forward draw. Cut a 15 cm sample and set it on a ruler or WPI gauge. Wrap the yarn closely but not tightly around the gauge for 2.5 cm and count the wraps. For a singles targeting DK-weight two-ply, aim for 16-18 wraps per 2.5 cm (which equals 16-18 WPI). If the count is higher (thinner), draft shorter; if lower (thicker), draft longer.
Step 2: Establish a consistent draft length

Once the test length is at target WPI, mentally note the draft length that produced it: roughly how far your back hand moves per drafting stroke. Try to repeat this distance consistently. Some spinners tape a small mark on their thigh or use a spot on their clothing as a visual reference for draft length. Consistency of motion produces consistency of yarn.

Stop spinning every 15 minutes and check a new length of yarn against the gauge. Tiredness, inattention, or a change in fibre preparation can cause drift in yarn thickness. Catching drift early allows correction before a substantial portion of the bobbin is spun at the wrong weight.

by moving the flyer hook across the bobbin after every 30-40 cm of spinning. This distributes the yarn evenly. An uneven cop with a large central mound spins differently toward the end because the bobbin is unbalanced.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems in consistent wheel spinning.

items.symptom: Yarn gets consistently thinner toward the end of the bobbin
items.cause: Fatigue changes the drafting rhythm, or the wheel draws in more aggressively as the bobbin fills and changes weight.
items.fix: Take regular breaks. Re-check and adjust scotch tension slightly as the bobbin fills.

items.symptom: Yarn looks consistent on the bobbin but is uneven in the finished skein
items.cause: The twist has not distributed evenly yet. Sections that looked thick had less twist and sections that looked thin had more.
items.fix: Set the twist thoroughly: long soak, firm snap, dry under tension. Measuring WPI from the finished, set skein rather than the bobbin is more accurate.
